steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changed the word into a noun by adding alif after second letter and changing final و to ء. This makes it mean the place that is high up.
Added ال with shadda on س to make it specific. The shadda appears because the ل of ال combines with the س.
Added و with fatha at beginning to mean 'and'
Added kasra at the end because this word is being used in an oath (By the sky!). The alif also gets lengthened to show emphasis in the oath.

steps.m: to knock or come at night

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added alif after first letter and kasra under second letter to show who does the action

steps.m: the one who comes at night
steps.n: Added ال at beginning to make it specific and doubled the ط with shadda

steps.m: and the one who comes at night
steps.n: Added و with fatha at beginning to mean 'and'

steps.m: and the night visitor (owned by السماءِ)
steps.n: Changed the alif to a special connecting alif and added a kasra mark at the end to show it belongs to 'the sky'
This is a question word used to ask about things or seek information
Added و at the beginning to connect this question with what came before it
The alif is elongated to help with pronunciation at the end of the word. This word is asking about الطَّارِقُ (the night-comer) in this ayah.

steps.m: to know deeply or be fully aware
steps.n: The root letters represent the basic concept of knowing
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters and show past tense
Added أَ at beginning to show causing someone else to know. Changed middle vowel to sukun.
Added alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end because the word ends with ي sound
Added كَ at the end to show the person being made to know is 'you'
This is the basic interrogative particle used to ask questions like 'what?' in English. In this ayah, it's asking about الطَّارِقُ (the night visitor).
Added fatha mark on م to show correct pronunciation

steps.m: to knock at night, to come as a night visitor
steps.n: The root represents the basic idea of knocking or pounding
Added fatha on ط and kasra on ر to create the active participle pattern. In Arabic culture, a طارق specifically refers to someone who comes at night, often unexpectedly.

steps.m: the night visitor (referring to a bright star)
steps.n: Added ٱل (the) at the beginning which causes the ط to get a shadda. Added damma at the end because this word is the subject of the sentence. In this Surah, الطارق refers specifically to a bright star that appears at night.

steps.m: to appear suddenly, to rise (like a star)
star (subject being described by الثاقب - the piercing)
steps.n: Added vowel marks to make the root letters into a noun meaning the thing that appears suddenly in the sky. This word is being described by الثاقب (the piercing) in this ayah.
Added 'the' to make it a specific star being talked about
Added damma to show this is the subject of the sentence
Connected the root letters together with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed from 'pierced' (past action) to 'piercing' (active/ongoing action) to describe how the star pierces through darkness. Like changing 'ran' to 'running'.

steps.m: the piercing, penetrating one (describes النَّجْمُ/the star)
steps.n: Added 'al' (the) at the beginning and changed the ending to show it's describing the star
This is a special word that doesn't come from a root. It's used at the start of sentences to emphasize what follows. In this ayah, it emphasizes that EVERY soul (كُلُّ نَفْسٍ) has a guardian over it.
Added damma and shadda to create a word meaning 'every'. This word describes نَفْسٍ (soul) in this ayah, meaning 'every soul'.
Added final damma to show this word's role as the subject of the sentence
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make it into a noun. This word is possessed by كُلُّ (every) in this ayah.
Added tanwin kasra (ٍ) at the end to show this is an indefinite noun being possessed
This is a special connecting word that shows one action happens right after another. In this ayah, it connects 'every soul' with 'has a guardian over it'.

steps.m: when (emphasizing the connection between كُلُّ نَفْسٍ and حَافِظٌ)
steps.n: Adding emphasis to the ل with a shadda shows that the connection between 'every soul' and 'having a guardian' is definite and certain.
This is a preposition showing something is on top of or over something else. In this ayah, it connects to حَافِظٌ (guardian) to show what the guardian is watching over.
Added ي with sukun (ْ) to prepare for attaching the pronoun. This is a helper letter that makes pronunciation easier.
Added ها which means 'her/it'. Here it refers back to نَفْسٍ (soul) which is feminine in Arabic.

steps.m: one who protects (subject of كُلُّ نَفْسٍ)
steps.n: Added alif after first letter and vowels to show this is someone who does the protecting. This word describes كُلُّ نَفْسٍ (every soul) in the ayah.
The double damma (ٌ) at the end shows this is indefinite, like saying 'a protector' instead of 'the protector'. It describes كُلُّ نَفْسٍ (every soul) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels to damma
Added ل to show this is a command/instruction
Added kasra at end to show this is a command/request
Added فَ at beginning to connect with previous context. This word's subject is ٱلْإِنسٰنُ (man/human) in this ayah.
Forms the word for 'human' - humans are social beings who live in communities, which connects to the root meaning of being social/friendly
Added ٱلْ to make it specific ('the human' instead of just any human) and ُ to show this is the subject (the one being told to look in فَلْيَنظُرِ)
Starting with the two separate words that combine to form this particle: مِن meaning 'from' and مَا meaning 'what'
When مِن combines with مَا, the ن assimilates into the م, shown by the shadda (ّ). The ن is completely absorbed into the م sound.
The final ا is dropped because it's followed by the verb خُلِقَ (was created). This مِمَّ asks 'from what thing was man created?'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: he was created (passive verb in فَلْيَنظُرِ ٱلْإِنسٰنُ مِمَّ خُلِقَ)
steps.n: Changed the vowels to show this is passive - meaning the person received the action instead of doing it. In this ayah, it's asking what was man created from.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: was created (subject of مِن مَّآءٍ دَافِقٍ)
steps.n: Changed the vowels to show this is passive - something was done to the subject rather than the subject doing something. This word is connected to the phrase مِن مَّآءٍ دَافِقٍ (from gushing water) which explains what humans were created from.
This is a basic preposition that shows where something came from or what it's made of. In this ayah, it connects to خُلِقَ (was created) to show what something was created from.
The root letters change to make the simple word 'water' - the middle letter و becomes ا and the last letter ه becomes ء (hamza)
Added basic vowel marks to show pronunciation
Added long alif to emphasize the word's meaning
Added tanween kasra to make it indefinite (like saying 'a water' vs 'the water')
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed the verb into an active participle (something that is doing the action). This describes the water (مَّآءٍ) mentioned in the ayah as something that gushes.

steps.m: gushing (describes مَّآءٍ in the genitive case)
steps.n: Added the tanwin kasra (two dots below) to show this word follows مَّآءٍ which comes after مِن (from). When a word comes after مِن, it gets this ending.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb

steps.m: he comes/goes out (from بَيْنِ ٱلصُّلْبِ وَٱلتَّرَآئِبِ - between the backbone and ribs)
steps.n: Adding يَ at the start and changing vowels shows this happens in the present. The sukun (ْ) on ر and damma (ُ) at the end are part of this present tense pattern. This word describes what comes out from between the backbone and ribs.
This is a basic preposition that shows where something comes from or starts. In this ayah, it connects the verb يَخْرُجُ (it comes out) to what follows.
Added kasra (ِ) under م to show how it's pronounced
Added a small ن mark (called tanwin) after the ن to show it's pronounced with an extra 'n' sound when connecting to the next word

steps.m: to separate or be between things

steps.n: Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to make it a preposition showing location between things
Added kasra at the end because this word connects to الصُّلْبِ (the backbone) in the ayah, showing what is between
Added damma and sukun to create the noun form meaning 'backbone' - something that is characteristically hard/firm
Added ٱل (the) at start, shadda on ص because it comes after ٱل, and kasra at end because it follows the preposition مِنۢ بَيْنِ (from between)
Adds special pattern to root 'dust/soil' to make a noun meaning one chest bone
Changes singular to plural form for multiple chest bones
Adds definite article and doubles the ت
Adds kasra at end to show possession
Adds conjunction to connect with previous phrase (الصُّلْبِ)
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what comes after it, like saying 'truly' or 'certainly' at the start of a sentence
Added هُ at the end to show who is being talked about. This connects to the word لَقَادِرٌ (capable) to say 'indeed he is capable'

steps.m: upon/over (shows Allah's power over رَجْعِهِۦ - bringing back)
steps.n: This is a common preposition showing position or authority over something. Here it connects to the word رَجْعِهِۦ (bringing back) to show Allah's complete power over this action.
The small alif (ٰ) at the end is just a pronunciation guide showing to make the ending sound longer. It doesn't change the meaning.
The root letters show the basic idea of returning or going back
Changed the verb into a noun meaning 'the act of returning'
Added هِ (his) to show possession. The word follows عَلَىٰ (upon) in the ayah.
The root letters show the basic idea of having power or ability
This pattern shows who has this quality. The word describes إِنَّهُ (indeed He) which refers to Allah in this ayah.

steps.m: surely having power/truly able (describes إِنَّهُ - indeed He)
steps.n: The لَ prefix adds emphasis, like saying 'truly' or 'certainly'

steps.m: surely having complete power/truly able in an absolute sense (describes إِنَّهُ - indeed He)
steps.n: The tanween dammah (ٌ) shows this is an unlimited or absolute quality, not restricted to specific instances. This fits the context of describing Allah's complete power.
Connected the root letters together with vowel marks to form the basic noun. The middle letter و stays as a consonant because it has sukoon.

steps.m: on the day (when تُبْلَى the secrets will be tested)
steps.n: Adding fatha at the end shows this word answers 'when?' about the testing of secrets mentioned after it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed to passive present tense to show this is happening to something rather than someone doing it. This word describes what happens on the Day mentioned in يَوْمَ.
Root letters س-ر-ر became the noun سَرّ by combining the two ر letters with a shadda
Changes from singular 'secret' to plural 'secrets' by adding ائِ in the middle of the word
Adds ٱل (the) and doubles the س with shadda because it comes after ٱل

steps.m: the secrets (subject of تُبْلَى - will be exposed)
steps.n: Adds ُ to show this is the subject of تُبْلَى (will be exposed/tested) and lengthens the alif to آ
This is the basic questioning word that asks about things or situations

steps.m: then what/so what (connects to previous statement about having no strength)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to connect this question to the previous statement, showing a logical sequence - after having no strength, then what?
Added fatha to show this is connecting to what comes after it
Added هُ to show who the word 'for/to' refers to
Added a small و mark (ۥ) which is just a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning
This is a basic preposition that shows something's source, origin, or what it's made of. Here it connects to قُوَّةٍ (strength/power) to show 'from/of strength/power'
Added damma (ُ) and doubled the middle letter to show strong emphasis. The ي is dropped because of the following changes.
The doubled و (shadda) emphasizes the strength. The ة makes it a noun meaning 'strength' rather than the action of being strong
The added marks (ٍ) at the end show this means 'any power' or 'a power of/belonging to' something else
This is the basic negation word that says 'no' or 'not' to something
Added و at the beginning to connect this negation to the previous phrase. Here it connects to say 'he has no strength and no helper'

steps.m: to help, assist, or give victory to
one who helps/assists (subject of negation with مِن)
steps.n: Added ا after first letter and ِ after second letter to show someone who does the action. This word follows مِن (from) in a negative sentence saying there is no helper.

steps.m: any helper (subject of negation with مِن)
steps.n: The tanween (ٍ) makes the word indefinite (any helper). It has this ending because it follows مِن in the phrase 'there is no helper'
Adds اء ending to make it a noun referring to something high/elevated
Added و at beginning to mean 'and'. This connects this word to what follows it (ذَاتِ ٱلرَّجْعِ - 'possessor of return').
Added ال to make it specific ('the sky') and doubled the س with shadda because it comes after ال

steps.m: and the sky (being described by ذَاتِ)
steps.n: Added kasra at end to show this word is being described by what follows it (ذَاتِ - 'possessor of')
This is the basic form of the word that shows possession or describes something having a quality. It's often used before another noun to mean 'having' or 'with'.
Added kasra (ِ) at the end because this word is describing 'the sky' (السَّمَآءِ) and comes between two connected words in the ayah.
Changed from basic root meaning 'return' into noun meaning 'the act of returning' by adding vowel marks
Added kasra at end to show this means 'of the return'
Added ال (the) at the beginning to make it 'the return' - the kasra ending shows it's connected to ذَاتِ (possessing) in the ayah
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make it a proper noun
Added ٱلْ to make it specific
Added وَ to connect it to what comes before, and ِ to show it's connected to the word ذَاتِ that comes after it
This is a special word that doesn't come from a root. It's used to show when something has or possesses a quality.

steps.m: possessor of (connects to الصَّدْعِ - the splitting)
steps.n: The kasra at the end shows this word is connected to الصَّدْعِ (the splitting) that comes after it, making the meaning 'possessor of splitting' or 'having splitting'
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make it a noun referring to the action itself
Added 'ال' to make it definite and added kasra at the end because it describes 'the earth' in this ayah
This is a common particle used for emphasis. In this ayah, it emphasizes that something is definitely a statement (قَوْلٌ)
The هُ suffix means 'it' and refers back to the Quran. This word connects to لَقَوْلٌ (statement) later in the ayah, telling us that the Quran is definitely a decisive statement.
The small و mark (ۥ) is just a pronunciation helper showing that the هُ should be pronounced clearly. It doesn't change the meaning.
This is the basic root meaning
Changed from basic root meaning 'say' into the noun 'speech' by adding the vowels a and silent w
Added double damma to make it indefinite nominative

steps.m: surely a speech/saying (emphasized statement after إِنَّهُۥ)
steps.n: Added لَ at the beginning to add emphasis, especially since it follows إِنَّهُۥ (indeed it)

steps.m: to separate or decide between things
steps.n: This is the basic root meaning
The root letters combine to form a noun meaning the result of separating or deciding
Added tanween (double damma) to show this is describing the word قَوْلٌ (statement) in the verse. It means the statement is one that decisively separates truth from falsehood.
This is a versatile particle that can function as a question word, negation, or relative pronoun depending on context
The و (and) connects this negation to what came before it. Together with the following phrase هُوَ بِٱلْهَزْلِ (it is for amusement), it means 'and it is not for amusement'

steps.m: he/it (referring to the Quran in this ayah)
steps.n: This is a standalone pronoun that refers back to something mentioned before. In this ayah, it refers to the Quran.

steps.m: to joke, jest, or not take seriously
steps.n: The root letters ه-ز-ل form a verbal noun meaning joking or jesting
Changed from basic root letters to a noun by adding the vowel marks - a fatha (َ) on ه and sukun (ْ) on ز makes it mean 'joking' as a thing rather than an action
Added ٱلْ at the beginning to mean 'the' and ِ at the end to show it's connected to the word بِ that comes before it

steps.m: with/by the joking (opposite of serious truth)
steps.n: Added بِ at the beginning to show this is describing how something is done. In this ayah, it's explaining that the Quran is not meant as a joke or something to take lightly.
This is a particle that adds emphasis to the statement يَكِيدُونَ (they are plotting)
Added هُمْ (them/they) to show who is being emphasized. This pronoun refers to the subject of يَكِيدُونَ (they are plotting)
Connected the root letters together to make a basic noun
Added يَ at start to show present tense, and changed vowels to show it's a verb
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. This word relates back to إِنَّهُمْ (indeed they) in the ayah.

steps.m: a secret plan (object of يَكِيدُونَ - they plot)
steps.n: Added vowel marks to turn the action into a noun. This word is what's being plotted in the phrase 'they plot a plot'.

steps.m: a secret plan (object of يَكِيدُونَ - they plot)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this is the object being plotted. This connects to the verb يَكِيدُونَ (they plot) to complete the meaning 'they plot a plot'.

steps.m: a secret plan (related to كَيْدًا in next ayah)
steps.n: Connected the root letters together and added vowel marks. This word is related to كَيْدًا (a plot/scheme) mentioned in the next ayah.
Changed from noun 'plot' to verb 'I plot' by adding أَ at start and changing vowels
The و at the start means 'and'

steps.m: to plan secretly or plot against someone

steps.n: Root letters k-y-d turned into a noun meaning 'plot/plan' by adding vowels. This is what's being plotted.
The ًا at the end shows this is what's being plotted. It's connected to the word أَكِيدُ (I am planning) in this ayah.
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb
Adds 'so/then' meaning and makes it a command
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed the word to show who does the action, like changing 'reject' to 'rejecter'

steps.m: those who reject/deny (acted upon by فَمَهِّلِ)
steps.n: Added ين to show we're talking about many people. This word is being acted upon by the command 'give respite to' (فَمَهِّلِ)
Added ال to specify these particular disbelievers, and َ at the end because it's being acted upon by the command 'give respite to'
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Added أَ at start and changed vowels to make it a command to one person (related to فَمَهِّلِ in previous ayah)

steps.m: give them time! (command to Prophet ﷺ about الْكٰفِرِينَ)
steps.n: Added هُمْ (them) to show who should be given time - refers back to الْكٰفِرِينَ (the disbelievers) mentioned in the previous ayah
Root letters were connected with vowels (u and a) to make a word meaning 'moving gently'
Added ي with sukun to make the word mean 'a little bit' or 'slightly'

steps.m: for a little while (describing أَمْهِلْ - grant respite)
steps.n: Added ًۢا at the end to make it an adverb that tells us to grant respite 'gently/slowly' for a short time
